NOD2.description: At the cellular level, this mutation affects the NOD2 protein, which plays a crucial role in immune system signaling. NOD2 is responsible for detecting bacterial components and triggering an immune response. When mutated, it can lead to dysregulated inflammation, contributing to conditions like Crohn’s disease. Eighty-eight percent of patients with NOD2 variants went into remission under treatment with AZA/6-MP, and 12% of patients with NOD2 variants were refractory to treatment with AZA/6-MP. The percentage of patients with NOD2 variants in remission under treatment with AZA/6-MP was significantly increased as compared to patients with NOD2 WT status (Fig. 1). In contrast, significantly more patients with NOD2 WT status were refractory to treatment with AZA/6-MP (Remicade) as compared to patients with NOD2 variants. Sources for this information can be found below.

CARD9.description: These lead compounds are confirmed to mimic the protective CARD9Δ11 mechanism that lost the key C‐terminal functions. These findings provide a novel CARD9‐targeting approach towards improved IBD therapeutics, and are still in the works.

MEFV.description: MEFV encodes pyrin, an inflammasome sensor. Gain-of-function mutations in MEFV can lead to excessive inflammation, contributing to IBD severity. There are currently no treatments available that have been developed for treatment of this gene. General treatments involving reducing inflammation can be taken to improve symptoms - talk with your doctor.

NCF4.description: This is a treatment currently being investigated. In this primary source, a patient underwent HSCT and improved without any conventional therapy for monogenic IBD. This may be an option for future patients with this mutation. NCF4 (Neutrophil Cytosolic Factor 4) mutations primarily affect the NADPH oxidase complex, which plays a crucial role in immune defense. Specifically, NCF4 encodes p40-phox, a subunit involved in reactive oxygen species (ROS) production within neutrophils. Altered ROS production can contribute to excessive inflammation, potentially influencing conditions like IBD (Inflammatory Bowel Disease).

SKAP2.description: There are currently no treatments available that have been developed for treatment of this gene. General treatments involving reducing inflammation can be taken to improve symptoms - talk with your doctor. SKAP2 is primarily expressed in immune cells, where it mediates cytoskeletal rearrangement, chemotaxis, and immune cell infiltration—processes that contribute to intestinal inflammation.

NOS2.description: There are currently no treatments available that have been developed for treatment of this gene. General treatments involving reducing inflammation can be taken to improve symptoms - talk with your doctor. Mutations can affect nitric oxide (NO) production, leading to altered immune responses and intestinal inflammation.

LACC1.description: There are currently no treatments available that have been developed for treatment of this gene. General treatments involving reducing inflammation can be taken to improve symptoms - talk with your doctor. Variants in LACC1 modulate T cell gene expression and metabolism, and LACC1 mutations correlate with lower expression in CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, potentially leading to T cell dysfunction and increased IBD risk.

LRRK2.description: This is a treatment still under development. It was used in mice, and mice treated achieved successful therapeutic effect. MLi-2 is an LRRK2 inhibitor. Although a higher inhibition of LRRK2 might be necessary to achieve full rescue, our results indicate that pharmacological inhibition of LRRK2 can be protective against colitis. LRRK2 mutations influence immune regulation, inflammation, and autophagy, which are critical pathways in both IBD and Parkinson's Disease pathogenesis.

ATG16L1.description: The dendritic cells of Crohn's disease patients treated with the MTOR inhibitor sirolimus (Brand name Rapamune) are able to effectively reverse the ability to degrade disease-associated bacteria.
